title: The Impact of Large Language Models on Open-Source Innovation: Evidence from GitHub Copilot
study_design_description: The study exploits a natural experiment around GitHub Copilot's launch in October 2021, where Copilot supported Python and Rust but not R and Haskell, creating an exogenous partition between otherwise comparable ecosystems. The main identification employs a Two-Way Fixed Effects (TWFE) model within a Difference-in-Differences framework using propensity score matching, with 1,187 Python packages matched to an equal number of R packages. The authors also estimate Synthetic Difference-in-Differences as an alternative specification and employ a difference-in-differences approach with temporal controls where the same Python packages serve as their own controls using data from an earlier period. A lead-lag model is employed to ensure the parallel trends assumption is not violated.
primary_task_description: Open-source software development where contributions are voluntary, modular, and digitally native, with coordination happening through commits, pull requests, and issue threads on shared platforms. Developers commit code changes accompanied by messages describing the changes. The open-source communities for these languages are hosted on GitHub, where contributors propose and implement code changes through commits.
population_description: For the primary analysis, 1,187 matched Python/R package pairs, aggregated at a quarterly level. The Python sample focused on the 2,000 most popular packages as of 2021; R packages were obtained from the Comprehensive R Archive Network (CRAN). Packages that had not been updated in the two years preceding Copilot's release were excluded (348 Python and 7,965 R packages). For robustness analysis, 1,373 matched Rust/Haskell pairs were used, starting from the top 2,000 packages on Crates.io and Hackage respectively. Data covers all repository activity from October 2019 to December 2022, including over 1.1 million commits, with an additional 272 thousand Python commits between 2016-2018 for temporal control.

arms.description: Python packages with access to GitHub Copilot from October 2021 onwards. GitHub Copilot, an LLM-powered coding assistance tool, initially supported Python from the start.

R packages without access to GitHub Copilot during the study period. R was not supported by Copilot; at least until the ChatGPT launch in late 2022, R packages did not have access to mainstream LLM coding assistance.
Rust packages with access to GitHub Copilot from October 2021 onwards. Rust is a performance-oriented language with a focus on type safety and systems programming.
Haskell packages without access to GitHub Copilot during the study period. Haskell is a performance-oriented language with a focus on type safety and systems programming.

outcomes.outcome_description: The number of new commits of the package in the quarter
Commits classified as substantive contributions where there is a positive net addition of functions across all files in the commit
Commits classified as incremental contributions without new function additions
Commits classified as code development (feature addition, code optimization)
Commits classified as maintenance (bug fixes, code cleanup, library dependency updates, deprecation, refactoring)
The number of version releases of the package in the quarter

The effect is consistent across specifications: 5.496 commits (31.89%) in the balanced panel and 4.894 commits (28.40%) in synthetic DiD.
The effect is consistent across specifications: 5.496 commits (31.89%) in the balanced panel and 4.894 commits (28.40%) in synthetic DiD.

notes: Study uses a natural experiment design exploiting GitHub Copilot's selective language support (Python/Rust supported; R/Haskell not supported) as of October 2021. Three complementary identification strategies employed: (1) cross-language DiD with propensity score matching (Python vs. R, 1,187 matched pairs), (2) temporal controls within Python packages comparing pre-Copilot era (2016-2018) to treatment era (2021-2022), and (3) replication with Rust vs. Haskell (1,373 matched pairs). Analysis period: October 2019 to December 2022 (ending before ChatGPT launch in late 2022). Two independent classification approaches for contribution types: (a) code-based function detection (commits with vs. without new function definitions), and (b) LLM-based commit message classification (maintenance, code development, documentation, testing, other). Main findings: Copilot increases total contributions by 28-40%, but incremental contributions (maintenance, non-function-adding) increase significantly more than substantive contributions (code development, function-adding). This differential is larger in high-activity projects and widens after the June 2022 model upgrade. Caveats: (1) Not all Python contributors necessarily used Copilot, so estimates likely represent lower bounds. (2) Findings bounded to October 2021-December 2022 period when Copilot was primary LLM code-completion tool. (3) Copilot offered code completion rather than interactive chat during study period. (4) Generalization beyond open-source requires caution due to self-selection and contributor autonomy. (5) Cannot directly observe cognitive processes of developers; mechanism (LLM reducing cognitive burden vs. self-selection into tasks where LLM is effective) remains open question. (6) Package-level data cannot capture heterogeneity across contributor types. Cohen's Kappa for LLM-based classification exceeded 0.6 for GPT-4o and DeepSeek, indicating substantial agreement.
